question: An order-fulfillment process needs three synchronous REST calls (payment, inventory, shipping) implemented as glue code. What is the docs' default recommendation for organizing this glue code?

options.text: Put all the glue code into one process-solution application that also deploys the BPMN model.
Build three separate applications — one worker per task type — even when there is no scaling or language reason to split.
Embed the REST calls in the BPMN XML as FEEL expressions on each service task so no workers are needed.
Run the workers inside the Zeebe broker as embedded plugins to avoid the network hop.

a.text: As a rule of thumb, the docs recommend implementing all glue code in one application — the so-called process solution — which often also deploys the BPMN model at startup. This makes the result self-contained and easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy.

b.text: The docs list splitting into multiple applications only as an exception (independent scaling/throttling or different programming languages). It is not the default.

c.text: BPMN service tasks call out to job workers; FEEL is for data, not for issuing REST calls. The docs do not propose this approach.

d.text: Zeebe runs no embedded user plugins for glue code; workers are external applications that activate jobs via the gateway API.

explanation: The docs' default recommendation is a single process solution: one application containing all the glue code (and often the BPMN itself), which is self-contained and easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy. Splitting into multiple applications is reserved for cases that need independent scaling/throttling or different programming languages.
